key_points:
## Concentration Risk Management — Directions, 2025 — Detailed Compliance Guide
*(RBI/DOR/2025-26/326, dated 2025-11-28)*

Limits how much an AIFI can be exposed to any one counterparty, group of connected counterparties, and the capital markets — to prevent a single failure from threatening the institution.

### Chapter I — Preliminary
- **Commencement:** immediate. **Applicability:** the five AIFIs (EXIM, NABARD, SIDBI, NHB, **NaBFID**).
- **Key definitions:** **Eligible capital base** = effective **Tier 1 capital** (per the Capital Adequacy Directions, last audited balance sheet; mid-year infusion allowed with auditor certificate to RBI). **Large Exposure** = total exposure to a counterparty/group **≥ 10%** of eligible capital base. **QCCP**, **CCP**, **clearing member**, **trade exposures**, **infrastructure lending** (Harmonized Master List) defined.

### Chapter II — Large Exposures Framework (the core limits)
- **Board policy** must set: refinance-portfolio exposure limits; the conditions for allowing exposure **beyond 20%** to a single counterparty; how connectedness is determined; and an internal ceiling for equity in non-financial enterprises. An **annual review** goes to the Board by **end-June** (September for NHB).
- **Scope:** applied at **consolidated (group)** and **solo** levels; covers counterparty exposures (not sectoral).
- **Exemptions** from the limits: the **refinance portfolio** (needs its own Board policy); GoI / State Government exposures eligible for 0% risk weight; RBI; GoI-guaranteed / GoI-security-backed exposures; intra-day bank exposures; QCCP clearing exposures.
- **Exposure ceilings (the headline numbers):**
  - **Single counterparty: 20%** of eligible capital base. The Board may allow **+5% (up to 25%)** with a specific Board-approved policy and **written exceptional reasons per case**. A further **+5% of Tier-1** is allowed where the extra exposure is an **infrastructure** loan/investment. **In no case may a single-counterparty exposure exceed 25%.**
  - **Group of connected counterparties: 25%** of eligible capital base, with **+10% of Tier-1** allowed for **infrastructure** loans/investments.
- **Connected counterparties** — group two+ parties as a single risk if either: (a) **Control** — one controls the other(s) or a common third party controls them (**>50% voting rights = automatic control**); or (b) **Economic interdependence** — assessed whenever exposure to one counterparty exceeds **5%** of eligible capital, using criteria such as ≥50% of one party's receipts/expenditure being with the other, cross-guarantees, single buyer/supplier dependence, or common funding source. The Directions give detailed one-way/two-way dependency and downstream/upstream contagion examples; a ring-fenced, bankruptcy-remote SPE can be excluded.
- **Exposure values:** computed per the Capital Adequacy method; off-balance-sheet items via CCFs with a **10% floor**; CRM-provider exposures recognised; special CDS treatment. **Bank exposures** (ex-refinance, ex-intra-day) capped at **25% of Tier-1**.
- **Look-Through Approach (LTA)** for funds/securitisations/InvITs/REITs: look through to underlying counterparties where an underlying exposure is **≥ 0.25%** of eligible capital; smaller underlyings can be assigned to the structure; unidentifiable underlyings go to a single aggregated **"unknown client."**
- **NBFC exposures:** subject to the single/group limits. **CCP exposures:** QCCP clearing exposures **exempt**; non-QCCP subject to the 25% limit; **segregated initial margin = 0** exposure.
- **Reporting & breaches:** monthly **Large Exposures return (Annex I)** to RBI (DOS) incl. the **20 largest exposures**; breaches are exceptional, must be **reported to RBI immediately**, and **no further exposure** is permitted until brought within limit; penalties may apply.

### Chapter III — Other Permitted Exposures & Prudential Limits
- **Sectoral internal limits** to ensure diversification.
- **Capital Market Exposure (CME):** aggregate CME (fund + non-fund, direct + indirect, solo & consolidated) **≤ 40% of net worth** (as of 31 March prior year), of which **direct ≤ 20%**.
- **Equity in a single company:** **≤ 49%** where made under statutory mandate, otherwise **≤ 10%**; as a pledgee up to 49%, but if acquired in satisfaction of claims, bring **below 10% within 3 years**. **Aggregate equity in non-financial enterprises ≤ 10% of net worth.**
- Detailed **exemptions** (financial-infrastructure entities like CCIL/NSDL/NPCI, Tier I/II bank instruments, debt MF units, infra-SPV promoter pledges, etc.) and computation rules (sanctioned vs outstanding, cost price for equity).

### Chapter IV — Repeal and Other Provisions
- Repeals prior AIFI concentration-risk instructions (circular dated 2025-11-28), with the standard saving clause; RBI's interpretation is final.

_Verify with the compliance team before acting._
implications:
**Why it matters for NaBFID:** This is one of the most operationally important directions for NaBFID. As a DFI writing very large infrastructure tickets, NaBFID will regularly press against the **single-counterparty (20%, up to 25%) and group (25%) limits**, and the **infrastructure carve-outs (+5% / +10% of Tier-1)** are precisely designed for its business. Getting the **connected-counterparty** grouping right — especially for project SPVs, sponsors, and EPC chains — is where concentration risk actually bites.

*Illustrative example:* If NaBFID lends to a power-project SPV and also to the sponsor group that controls it, those exposures must be aggregated as a **group of connected counterparties** (control via >50% voting is automatic) and kept within 25% of eligible capital — unless the extra exposure is infrastructure lending, which allows up to +10% of Tier-1. A ring-fenced, bankruptcy-remote SPV may be assessed separately. NaBFID must also file the monthly Large Exposures return listing its 20 largest exposures (RBI/DOR/2025-26/326, dated 2025-11-28).

_Verify with the compliance team before acting._
